body: On the product detail page, inspect whether the product specification sheet (if present) is structured for scannability: specs should be grouped into semantically related sections (e.g., 'Dimensions', 'Connectivity', 'Compatibility'), presented in a single-column layout rather than a multi-column grid, and use visual aids such as alternating row background colors or clear typographic hierarchy to help users quickly trace from a spec label to its corresponding value. Positive signals include grouped spec sections with clear section headings, alternating row shading or a visual separator between label and value, and a brief summary or 'Key Specs' callout that highlights the two to four most decision-critical specifications near the top of the spec table. Negative signals include an undifferentiated flat list of specifications without semantic grouping (a 'wall of specs'), a multi-column spec layout where label-to-value alignment is ambiguous, an absence of any visual differentiation between rows, or critical specs (such as compatibility or dimensions) buried deep in an alphabetically sorted list without a summary callout. When violations are found, recommend grouping specs into 3–6 labelled sections, applying alternating row shading, and adding a 'Key Specs' summary block above the full table. Baymard Institute's product spec sheet research found that 50% of e-commerce sites have spec sheet designs that are difficult for users to scan, with 23% failing to group specs into sections and 20% using multi-column layouts that create alignment confusion.

provenance.citation_text: 50% of e-commerce sites have spec sheet designs that are difficult for users to scan. The research identifies critical design problems: 23% of e-commerce sites don't group specs semantically into sections, and 20% of sites use multiple columns in the spec sheet. Additionally, only 3% of sites use a summary to highlight the most critical product specs, and 23% of sites don't use visual aids like background colors or icons to improve scannability. The study emphasizes that ungrouped specifications create a problematic wall of specs, forcing users to expend significant effort locating information. The research demonstrates through eye-tracking studies that visual styling techniques — particularly alternating row colors — enable users to more quickly and reliably connect specification labels with their corresponding values.

ai_review.reviewer_persona: neural-heuristic-reviewer v1.0 (top-1% senior CRO consultant; 20yr; multi-vertical D2C/SaaS/marketplace/B2B/lead-gen/fintech/media)
ai_review.why_generated: Drafter targeted Baymard's PDP spec-sheet-scannability research (50% sites with unscannable spec sheets — grouping/single-column/visual-aids gaps) — Baymard's documented PDP-detail UX finding.
ai_review.how_reviewed: URL matches Baymard spec-sheet research (HTTP 200); 50% gap consistent with Baymard PDP-detail benchmarks; grouping + single-column recommendations are canonical Baymard guidance; rule+incremental classification accurate.

dimension_findings.finding: URL matches Baymard /blog/spec-sheet-scannability pattern; HTTP 200 verified
50% unscannable-spec-sheets stat consistent with Baymard PDP-detail benchmark
pdp-only correctly limited; spec-bearing product categories (electronics/appliances) most affected
no conversion-rate predictions; phrasing is observational/recommendation-form
quantitative value=50 derivable from citation; metric methodology defensible
grouping + single-column + visual-aids interventions are concrete + executable
